petitioner_background: The petitioner sought classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in the arts pursuant to section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act. The record contains limited information about her specific field or nationality, but references photographs of trophies, awards with foreign language writing, membership in organizations, a published interview, movie credits, and employment as evidence of her career. The director and AAO previously determined she had not established the sustained national or international acclaim necessary for the classification.
outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the motion to reopen as untimely. The AAO had dismissed the petitioner's appeal on October 26, 2009, and the petitioner submitted the motion to the AAO (rather than to the Texas Service Center as required) on November 18, 2009. The AAO returned the motion on November 20, 2009, and the petitioner properly filed it with the Texas Service Center on November 30, 2009, beyond the 33-day window for mailed decisions under 8 C.F.R. § 103.5a(b). Given the clear instructions on the cover page of the initial AAO decision and the regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(1)(iii)(E) directing motions to the office maintaining the record, the petitioner failed to demonstrate that her untimely filing was beyond her control or due to USCIS error. Additionally, the new evidence submitted on motion — letters not from award-issuing authorities and photographs with self-serving captions — was insufficient, and the petitioner failed to provide certified translations of foreign language writing on awards as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3).
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Matter of Obaigbena, 19 I&N Dec. 533, 534 n.2 (BIA 1988)
Matter of Laureano, 19 I&N Dec. 1, 3 n.2 (BIA 1983)
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg'l. Comm'r. 1972)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The motion to reopen was dismissed as procedurally defective and untimely. The petitioner initially submitted the motion to the wrong office (the AAO instead of the Texas Service Center that maintained the record), and by the time it was properly refiled with the Texas Service Center on November 30, 2009, the 33-day filing deadline for mailed decisions had expired. The petitioner also failed to submit certified English translations of foreign language writing on awards as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3). The AAO also noted that unsupported assertions of counsel do not constitute evidence and that going on record without supporting documentary evidence is insufficient to meet the burden of proof.
